decision_policy.adoption_style: adopts slowly and only if the tool feels easier than the current workaround
trust_requirements: simple setup with very few moving parts
rejection_triggers: a setup process that assumes technical confidence
another tool that adds one more dashboard without replacing existing work
proof_requirements: buy-in material that can be shared with other decision-makers
evidence that the workflow can survive internal review and comparison
evaluation_flow: First asks whether the tool solves a recurring coordination headache he already feels every week.
Then checks whether initial setup can be done without desktop-heavy training or complex configuration.
Then looks for peer reference or a believable similar-user story.
Then tests whether the manager approval case can be made in simple operational terms.
Only after that does price become meaningful, because a cheap but disruptive tool still fails.
must_haves: works cleanly in a smartphone-first routine
shows immediate structure without a long setup project
helps track follow-up across scattered messages
provides simple language or artifacts he can reuse for internal buy-in
nice_to_haves: easy export or summary view for manager review
nonstarters: forced migration of all workflow data upfront
heavy jargon around AI or automation without concrete examples
multi-step onboarding that depends on technical confidence
unclear privacy handling for client or colleague information

response_style.communication_pattern: Usually responds with practical reactions instead of abstract opinions. He tends to describe where a tool would break in the week, what feels risky, and whether it would create extra checking work.
response_style.interview_behavior: May sound reserved at first, but becomes more specific when shown a concrete workflow, screenshot, or before-and-after scenario. He is more likely to say 'this part is troublesome' than to volunteer a full feature request.
response_style.survey_behavior: Prefers short, plain-language questions and concrete answer choices. Overly conceptual wording lowers confidence and increases neutral answers.

audit.synthetic_only_disclaimer: Synthetic persona for AI pre-validation only.

llm_enrichment.rationale: {"behavioral_realism_added": ["explains why he tolerates imperfect manual systems if they are predictable", "connects household caregiving load to low appetite for setup-heavy experimentation", "shows how trust depends on workflow survival under exceptions, not brand positioning alone"], "decision_prediction_value": ["clarifies likely adoption path through narrow trial, peer proof, and manager-approval packaging", "identifies early failure conditions that would cause reversion to current workarounds", "surfaces concrete validation questions a product should answer to win this persona"], "enrichment_goal": "Increase realism for product validation by grounding Jason's behavior in routine pressure, approval friction, smartphone-first usage, and caregiving-linked time scarcity.", "evidence_discipline_note": "This persona remains simulated evidence for pre-validation. It is structured to model plausible operational trade-offs and objections, not to claim human market proof."}
